coredata.dc:title: Designing a modified electrode based on graphene quantum dot-chitosan application to electrochemical detection of epinephrine
coredata.dc:creator: Tashkhourian, Javad
Nami-Ana, Sayedeh Fatemeh
Shamsipur, Mojtaba
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots were used for constructing modified carbon paste electrode in a simple way using chitosan as a stabilizing agent. Since these dots are soluble in aqueous solution, chitosan is used to keep the quantum dot into the paste. A triple mixture nanocomposite including graphene quantum dots surrounded by chitosan on the plane of graphite was formed. The graphene quantum dots have plenty of carboxyl groups with the negative charges, which can have a strong electrostatic interaction with amine functional groups in chitosan. This characteristic besides having conjugated π-π bonding makes a stable and an excellent electrochemical platform for epinephrine detection. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and cyclic voltammetry showed that the prepared graphene quantum dots-chitosan modified carbon paste electrode possesses a fast electron transfer compared with the bare electrode and a noticeable electrocatalytic activity toward electrooxidation of epinephrine. Under optimized experimental conditions, the modified electrode revealed a linear relationship between the peak current and concentrations of epinephrine over a wide range from 0.36 to 380 μM with the limit of detection of 0.3 nM (3Sb/m). The modified electrode, proposed as a new promising nanoplatform is suitable for designing of electrochemical sensors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quantum dots
Carbon nanomaterials
Bulk-modified electrode
Hydrodynamic chronoamperometry

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Voltammetric behavior of epinephrine on the GQD-CS-CPE modified electrode
Calibration plot, limit of detection reproducibility, and repeatability

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Shiraz Institute for Cancer Research, Shiraz University of Medical Sciences

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge the support of this work by Shiraz University Research Council (grant number 93GCU2M172254 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of GQDs solution prepared in Section 2.3, (A) XRD pattern, (B) UV–Vis absorption spectrum with inset showing the fluorescence spectrum at λex of 365 nm, (C) TEM image of GQDs, (D) FTIR spectrum of GQDs, (E) FTIR spectrum of GQDs–chitosan mixture.
Cyclic voltammograms of 5.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3− in KCl (0.1 M) recorded on the bare CPE (a), GQDs–CPE (b), CS-CPE (c) and GQDs–CS-CPE (d).

SEM images of Chitosan-graphite powder prepared by hand mixing (A), Chitosan-graphite powder prepared by ultrasonic mixing (B), CS-GQD-graphite powder prepared by hand mixing (C), CS-GQD-graphite powder prepared by ultrasonic mixing (D), and smaller scale TEM images of CS-GQD-graphite powder prepared by ultrasonic mixing (E and F).

A. EIS of 5.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− recorded on the bare CPE (a), the GQD–CPE (b), CS-CPE (c) and GQD-CS-CPE (d). Inset: magnification of (c) and (d). B. The Bode plot of bare and modified electrodes. Inset shows the circuit model used for fitting.

A Cyclic voltammograms of 0.5 mM epinephrine in phosphate buffer solution (0.1 M, pH 7.0) recorded on the bare CPE (a), GQD-CPE(b), CS-CPE (c) and GQD-CS/CPE (d) at a scan rate 100 mV s−1. Inset: back ground current at GQD–CS/CPE. B. Square wave voltammograms of 0.5 mM epinephrine in PB (0.1 M, pH 7.0) recorded on the bare CPE (a) and the GQD–CS/CPE (b).
Cyclic voltammograms of 5.0 × 10−5 M of EP in PB of pH 7.0 at GQD-CS/CPE at different scan rates from 10 to 500 mV s−1 (A). Plots of peak current vs. scan rate (B) and square root of scan rate (C).

(A) Hydrodynamic chronoamperograms of various concentrations of EP at GQD-CS/CPE. (B) Corresponding calibration curve of current vs. EP concentration. a) 0.35, b) 3.9, c) 9.9, d) 31.8, e) 51.8, f) 63.5, g) 75.4, h) 87.2, i) 99.0, j) 118.5, k) 138.0, l) 157.4, m) 176.1, n) 196.0, o) 215.2, p) 234.3, q) 272.3, r) 310.0, s) 347.7. t) 384.6.
Comparison of some characteristics of GQD-CS carbon paste modified electrode with some previously reported modified electrodes for the determination of EP.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: DTT: dodecanethiol; ILs: ionic liquid; TGA: thioglycolic-acid; CS: chitosan; MWCNT: multi-walled carbon nanotube; OMCs: ordered mesoporous carbon; GO: graphene oxide. PBCBN: poly(brilliant cresyl blue) nanocomposite; 2PHC: 2-(4-oxo-3-phenyl-3,4-dihydro-quinazolinyl)-N′-phenyl-hydrazinecarbothioamide; EPPGE: edge plane pyrolytic graphite electrode; PPy: polypyrrole; Oppy: over-oxidized polypyrrole; CM: poly curcumin (1,7 Bis ((4-hydroxy-3-methoxyphenyl)-1,6-heptadiene-3,5 dione), poly CM); GQD: graphene quantum dot. CV: cyclic voltammetry, Ampero: amperometry, SWV: square wave voltammetry, DPV: differential pulse voltammetry.

head.ce:title: Designing a modified electrode based on graphene quantum dot-chitosan application to electrochemical detection of epinephrine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Shiraz University, Shiraz 71456, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Razi University Kermanshah, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots were used for constructing modified carbon paste electrode in a simple way using chitosan as a stabilizing agent. Since these dots are soluble in aqueous solution, chitosan is used to keep the quantum dot into the paste. A triple mixture nanocomposite including graphene quantum dots surrounded by chitosan on the plane of graphite was formed. The graphene quantum dots have plenty of carboxyl groups with the negative charges, which can have a strong electrostatic interaction with amine functional groups in chitosan. This characteristic besides having conjugated π-π bonding makes a stable and an excellent electrochemical platform for epinephrine detection. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and cyclic voltammetry showed that the prepared graphene quantum dots-chitosan modified carbon paste electrode possesses a fast electron transfer compared with the bare electrode and a noticeable electrocatalytic activity toward electrooxidation of epinephrine. Under optimized experimental conditions, the modified electrode revealed a linear relationship between the peak current and concentrations of epinephrine over a wide range from 0.36 to 380 μM with the limit of detection of 0.3 nM (3Sb/m). The modified electrode, proposed as a new promising nanoplatform is suitable for designing of electrochemical sensors.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nano-materials such as nano-diamonds, carbon nanotubes, graphene sheets and graphene quantum dots (GQD) possess unique and novel properties with great potential applications in various fields [1]. Graphene is a single layer of carbon atoms with a high specific surface area, high porosity, high chemical stability, and excellent electron-transport properties [2].

GQDs are small graphene fragments, in which the electronic transports are confined in all three dimensions. They are a type of zero-dimensional (0D) materials which derive their characteristics from graphene. GQDs are another novel type of carbon nano-material with sizes below 10 nm [3–7].

When the two-dimensional (2D) graphene sheets are converted into 0D GQD, due to quantum confinement and edge effects, they will exhibit unusual properties such as energy-related applications and tunable luminescence behavior [8, 9]. In the past few years, there has been an intensive attention to 0D GQD in the literature [10, 11], especially because they are strongly luminescent and well-dispersed in various solvents [11], and as extensively discussed in the literature, possess many superior physical and chemical properties [12–14].

Epinephrine (EP) is one of the important natural catecholamine neurotransmitters in central nervous system. The functioning of epinephrine in the central nervous, cardiovascular, and hormonal systems is of vital importance [15]. The patients with Parkinson's disease have low levels of epinephrine, while its excess amount in the body affects heart rate, blood pressure, lipolysis, immune system, and glycogen metabolism [16]. A large number of analytical methods such as high-performance liquid chromatography, capillary electrophoresis, and mass spectrometry for the determination and detection of EP have been reported so far [17]. However, although these methods exhibit high sensitivity and selectivity, they are challenging because of high costs, complicated sample pretreatment and timely analysis. In contrast to these complicated techniques, the electrochemical methods with their simple, fast, portable field-based and cost-effective nature have attracted considerable attention in analytical performances. A number of carbon pastes, gold and glassy carbon electrodes modified with different nanostructures have been introduced to detect epinephrine, in the past decade [18–21].

Despite the unique advantages of graphene quantum dots [5, 11] there has been no former report of them being used for modification of carbon paste electrodes, to the best of our knowledge. The high solubility of GQDs in aqueous solution is a significant advantage to functionalize and apply them in spectroscopic methods. However, this property limits their direct use to prepare modified electrodes (especially, modified carbon paste electrodes), because of the increasing possibility of leaking from the electrode into the solution and a consequent decrease in the stability of the electrode. Meanwhile, chitosan (CS) is well-known as a weakly alkaline natural polymer that is soluble in dilute aqueous acetic acid solution, as its glucosamine unit (R-NH2) is protonated (R-NH2 +) in this medium. Chitosan possesses distinct chemical and biological properties [22] due to the presence of reactive amino and hydroxyl groups in its linear chains, which enables the polymer to be modified chemically [23–25]. Thus, in this work, we expect that CS can help to keep GQD in the paste mixture, via the strong electrostatic interaction between the positively charged CS and negatively charged GQD.

In this work, we used GQD as a modifier to construct bulk-modified carbon paste electrodes via simple ultrasonic mixing without needing any electrochemical deposition. An optimized ternary mixture of GQD solution, CS solution and, graphite powder was used to prepare carbon paste modified electrode in order to overcome the solubility problem of GQD in aqueous sample solutions. Although high surface area and good electron transfer capability of CS make it a fascinating candidate for electrode modification, CS shows a poor electrochemical activity. Applying ultrasonic to blend the mixture formed a novel nanocomposite particle of CS-GQD on graphite layer. These triple nanocomposites have the biocompatibility of chitosan (CS) and excellent conductivity of GQD and graphite [24]. It can wrap multicores of GQD and form a bigger nanocomposite on graphite layer because of our fabrication technique. In traditional hand mixing, we found no remarkable behavior. In addition, we construct a triple nanocomposite including chitosan-GQD-graphite that has been directly used to be mixed with paraffin as a novel carbon content of paste electrode. It is a good nanoplatform paste electrode (bulk-modified electrode); it is also capable of modifying other material or quantum dots especially the quantum dots that are soluble and have the possibility of leakage at the electrode/solution interface.

In order to investigate the electrochemical behavior of this newly modified carbon paste electrode (MCPE), epinephrine (EP) has been studied. The resulting electrode shows noticeable electrocatalytic behavior for electrooxidation of EP. The modified electrode has then been applied to the detection of epinephrine as an important biological compound in real samples. Moreover, since the prepared modified electrode possesses a good electrochemical performance, it can be proposed as a new promising nanoplatform for designing of electrochemical and biosensors.

All electrochemical measurements were carried out on an Autolab electroanalyzer Model PGSTAT302N (Metrohm Autolab B·V, Utrecht, The Netherlands). All the voltammetric measurements were carried out in a three-electrode system using the modified carbon paste electrode as a working electrode, an Ag/AgCl reference electrode and a Pt wire counter electrode. Measurements of pH were made with a Metrohm 780 pH meter. The transmission electron micrographs (TEM) of the GQDs were obtained by a transmission electron microscope (Zeiss, model EL10C) operating at an accelerating voltage of 80 kV. SEM analysis was performed on a scanning electron microscope (Philips XL30 at an accelerating voltage of 25 kV). All fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Perkin-Elmer LS50B spectrofluorometer, using 1-cm quartz cuvettes. The UV–Vis absorbance spectra were collected using an Ultrospec 4000 (Pharmacia Biotech, UK) spectrophotometer with matched 1.0-cm quartz cells (Helma cell). The ultrasound irradiation was carried out by an Ultrasonic Processor (Heilscher, UP200H, Germany). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of GQD in aqueous solutions were obtained using an FTIR spectrometer (spectrum RXI, PerkinElmer) equipped with CaF2 circular cell windows. The XRD patterns were recorded on a Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer using the Cu Kα radiation (40 kV, 300 mA) of 0.154 nm. CHN elemental analysis was carried out using an organic elemental analyzer (Vario MICRO).

Paraffin oil and graphite powder (particle size <100 μm) were supplied by Fluka. Citric acid (CA), NaOH, NaH2PO4·H2O and Na2HPO4, H3PO4, KCl, K3/K4Fe(CN)6, chitosan, epinephrine, sugars and amino acids (all from Merck, Dermastat Germany) were of the highest purity available and used without any further purification. All the solutions were prepared using double distilled water. A stock solution of EP (0.01 M) was freshly prepared for each measurement. 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution (PB) was used as the supporting electrolyte in all experiments. A stock aqueous solution containing 1 mg mL−1 of chitosan powder dissolved in 3% acetic acid was applied to disperse GQD required to the fabrication of the MCPE.

According to Dong's method [25], an easy bottom–up procedure was used to synthesize GQD via carbonization of citric acid (CA). In a typical procedure, 2 g CA was put into a 25 mL beaker and heated up to 200 °C using an electrical drying oven. After 5 min, the CA was melted. The color of the liquid was subsequently changed from colorless to pale yellow and then converted to orange in 30 min, implying the formation of GQD. The orange liquid was obtained and added drop by drop into 100 mL of 10 mg mL−1 NaOH solution, under vigorous stirring, and was neutralized to pH 7.0. Finally, 100.0 mL of the GQD aqueous solution was obtained. A dialysis bag (molecular weight cutoff = 1000) was used for 48 h in order to separate the big particles, after that, it was stored at 4 °C before use. Vacuum freeze-drying was used to calculate the stock solution concentration, which was obtained 25 mg mL−1.

The unmodified CPE was prepared by hand mixing of graphite powder with paraffin oil at a ratio of 65:35 (w/w) in an agate mortar. For optimization purposes, the amounts of GQD and chitosan were changed to construct the modified electrode with the best performance. 65.0 mg of the graphite powder was mixed with 1.2 mL of GQD stock solution and 10.0 mL of chitosan stock solution, under optimized conditions for preparation of the GQD-CS modified electrode. Chitosan, due to its cationic nature in acidic solution, can be used as a surfactant. Then GQD including 10 mg mL−1 of NaOH solution was added to the CS and graphite dispersion. Finally, the pH value increased and chitosan electrostatically interacted with negative GQD on the surface of graphite layer into the solution. The ternary mixture was dispersed by the ultrasonic probe in ambient conditions for 15 min. Then the homogeneous solution was dried in a vacuum drying oven at 80 °C to dryness. Finally, the dried powder mixture including GQD, CS and graphite powder was mixed with 39 mg of paraffin oil by hand mixing in an agate mortar to prepare the modified carbon paste electrode. A certain amount of each prepared paste was put into plastic syringe tubes (internal radius 2.2 mm) to construct both unmodified and modified CPEs. The electrical contact of the paste was established by putting a silver wire into the plastic syringe tube. The fresh electrode surface was obtained when a small part of the paste pressed out; it was polished with fax paper so that a smooth surface was acquired.

A certain volume of epinephrine stock solution (0.01 M) and 10 mL 0.1 M PB was added into an electrochemical cell, and then the three-electrode system was installed on it. The cyclic voltammetry was performed at a scan rate of 100 mV s−1 in the range of −0.30 to 0.90 V. Square wave voltammograms were recorded from −0.10 to 0.90 V with the following parameters: increment potential, 0.004 V; pulse amplitude, 0.05 V; pulse width, 0.05 s; frequency 10 Hz. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy was carried out at a polarization potential of 0.10 V vs. Ag/AgCl (3 mol L−1 KCl) in the frequency range of 0.01 to 105 Hz.

The blood serum sample was obtained from the Shiraz Blood Transfusion Organization (Iran). Samples were stored at 4 °C in a refrigerator. A given amount of the serum sample (5 mL) was added to the phosphate buffer solution (100 mL) and used for the determination of EP without any further pretreatment. The epinephrine hydrochloride injection solution was purchased from a drugstore. Each milliliter of single dose injection solution contained 1.0 mg epinephrine together with some added sodium chloride and sodium metabisulfite. A given amount of injection solution was diluted with the phosphate buffer solution and used as a real sample in chronoamperometry study using a standard addition method. Then a given volume of pure epinephrine solution was injected into this solution and the chronoamperometric procedure was followed.

CHN elemental analysis of GQD is showed in Table S1. As it is obvious, the carbon content is higher than that of CA, implying that CA is carbonized in some degree during the pyrolysis, as it has been reported in the literature [25]. The XRD patterns of GQDs (Fig. 1A), show a wide peak around 2θ = 30.0°, which can be attributed to the (002) planes of the hexagonal graphite structure that is consisted with 0.34 nm (distance between the graphitic plane), suggesting that carbonizing CA produced graphite structures. As is obvious, the XRD pattern does not show any sharp peak of the GQDs, most possibly due to the presence of oxygenated functional groups that lead to a disordered structure of GQDs. Consequently, the structure shows the lack of considerable ordering in the graphene quantum dots, as it compared to that of graphite. As seen in Fig. 1B, the UV–Vis spectrum showed two absorption peaks, a sharp absorption band around 250 nm showing π-π* transition and an absorption band at 340 nm attributing to n-π* transition. The inset of this figure shows that the solution of GQDs emits blue light at 470 nm when excited with 365 nm, as reported before [26].

The morphology of GQD, as characterized by TEM (Fig. 1C), clearly displayed the presence of some nanosheets below 10 nm in size (about 3 nm). FTIR was used to characterize the GQD (Fig. 1D). Obviously, the absorption of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups in the FTIR spectrum of GQD exhibited that GQD contains the COOH group. Additionally, the GQD showed absorption of stretching vibration of CH at 2950 cm−1 and that below 1350 cm−1 (COO−), suggesting that the GQD contain some incompletely carbonized CA. Meanwhile, the interaction of GQD with chitosan was also investigated by FTIR spectra (Fig. 1E). The spectrum showed a signal at 1211 cm−1, demonstrating that the amidation has occurred between graphene quantum dots and CS molecules, as had been reported before [23].

Electrochemical characterization of the modified electrode was carried out using [Fe(CN)6]3− as a suitable probe. The electrochemical behavior of different modified electrodes was investigated with cyclic voltammetry (CV) in a 5.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− solution containing 0.1 M KCl as a supporting electrolyte and the results are showed in Fig. 2 . As it is obvious, at the bare CPE (a), a pair of redox peaks with a quite small intensity and a ΔE p of about 470 mV have appeared. While, in the case of the GQD–CPE (b), the intensity was increased and the peak-to-peak potential decreased to 300 mV, indicating a higher electrical conductivity of GQD. At the CS-CPE (c), the current intensity significantly increased and the peak-to-peak potential (ΔE p) decreased to 200 mV, which could be attributed to the high affinity of positively charged chitosan to negatively charged [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− probe [22, 23]. Finally, at the GQD-CS-CPE modified electrode (d), there was a little increasing in peak-to-peak separation, it might be because of decreasing positive charge of chitosan in the presence of GQD with the negative charge which decreases the affinity of negative [Fe(CN)6]3− to less positive CS-GQD nanocomposite than chitosan alone. Nevertheless, as compared with the electrodes modified with either GQD or CS alone, the redox peaks showed a further increase which most possibly be due to the excellent electrical conductivity of the GQD–CS, and enhanced surface area of the electrode. The values of the electroactive surface area for the modified electrode and the bare electrode were calculated 3.98 and 0.57 cm2, according to Randles-Sevcik equation, respectively.

Hand mixing and ultrasonic mixing were used to blend the carbonic components of paste electrode. As showed in the SEM images of CS-CPE (Fig. 3A and B), when chitosan was only used in the presence of graphite, there was no nanostructure on graphite layer not by hand mixing nor ultrasonic mixing, indeed, CS cannot be dispersed as homogenous particles on the surface in these cases. When chitosan has been used in the presence of GQD and graphite by hand mixing, no nanostructure was formed either (Fig. 3C).

However, a mixture of GQD–CS-graphite showed a homogeneous structure composing of nanoparticles composite at the surface of graphitic layers (Fig. 3D) only when ultrasonic has been used for fabrication. The average size of these composite nanoparticles was about 200 nm. Traditional hand mixing did not form any observable nanoparticles; however, the composite nanoparticles were formed during use of ultrasonic mixing. As presented in TEM image showed in Fig. 3C GQD had the size about 3 nm, which was much less than that of the composite nanoparticles (Fig. 3D). Probably these big nanocomposite particles including multicores of GQD, which have been wrapped by chitosan and form a bigger nanocomposite on graphite layer due ultrasound fabrication. Here, in the case of TEM results (Fig. 3E and F), an enlarged nanocomposite particle in SEM image (Fig. 3D) is highlighted and even showed a good dispersion of graphene quantum dots with sizes below 10 nm within bigger particles on the graphitic layer. There is not a good contrast because of the carbonic nature of these triple nanocomposites. However, the edge of graphite and the GQD core is clearly obvious in (Fig. 3E).

The above-mentioned results have been also confirmed by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). Fig. 4A shows the typical Nyquist plots for the bare and modified CPEs in the presence of 5.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− in 0.1 M KCl. The bare CPE (curve a) shows a higher electron transfer resistance (large semicircle) indicating that the electron transfer rate is very slow. At the GQD-CPE (curve b), the Nyquist plot shows a smaller semicircle compared to the bare electrode, which exhibits a lower interfacial electron transfer resistance. Therefore, it can be concluded that the GQD possess a great potential to decrease the charge transfer resistance at the electrode surface. This result is consistent with the previous report [27]. While, in the case of the GQD–CS modified electrode (curve d), the semicircle becoming very small reveals the fact that the charge transfer resistance has decreased extremely, clearly indicating that the presence of chitosan and GQD can significantly enhance the conductivity and facilitate the electron transfer between probe and electrode interface. However, when chitosan alone was used for electrode modification (curve c), the resulting CS-CPE showed a significant decrease in the semicircle, despite the fact that CS has been considered as a nonconductive polymer. In fact, this result can be attributed to the electrostatic interaction between the positively charged chitosan and the negatively charged [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− probe and high affinity of chitosan to iron ions. The Kramers-Kronig (KK) relationships [28] were then used to assess the validity of measured impedance (Fig. S1) and the results showed a good agreement.

The equivalent electrical circuit used to fit the EIS data (Fig. 4 inset) and the charge transfer resistance (R ct) was obtained 449, 126, 48.8, and 26 Ω for the bare CPE, GQD-CPE, Cs-CPE, and QGD-CS-CPE, consequently.
a )
R ct
=
RT
nF
i 0
b )
R ct
=
RT
n 2
F 2
A
ik 0
C

The rate constant of electron transfer (K 0) was calculated from b equation for the bare and modified electrode [29]. This value is about one-third of which was calculated from the CV data (calculated based on Laviron equation); however, the relation between k 0 at the bare and modified electrode in two analysis technique remains constant; k 0 for electron transfer at modified electrode was 10 times better than the bare electrode in both methods (CV and EIS).

The capacitive (charge storage) or resistance to charge transfer behavior of the modified electrode is characterized by the Bodes plots (Fig. 4B). The GQD-CPE, Cs-CPE, and GQD-Cs-CPE shows phase angles about 38.2, 32.1, and 30.6 respectively. These phase angles are much lower than the unmodified electrode (66.02). Further results with the Nyquist data implied that modified electrode would demonstrate a fast charge transfer process. Therefore, the lower the phase angle, the higher charge transfer ability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Voltammetric behavior of epinephrine on the GQD-CS-CPE modified electrode
ce:section.ce:para: Cyclic voltammetry has been used for investigating the electrochemical behavior of EP (0.5 mM solution) at the surface of CPE, CS-CPE, GQD-CPE, and GQD-CS-CPE and the results are showed in Fig. 5A, at a bare CPE (a), a pair of redox peaks have appeared. At GQD-CPE (b), the redox peaks increase in intensity. Here, epinephrine can form a π-π stacking interaction through its phenyl moiety with the long-range π-conjugation of GQD [26, 27]. At the GQD-CPE (curve b), the response signals are further improved by their negatively shifting oxidative potential, indicating that GQD-CPE possesses a good electrocatalytic ability to the electrochemical reaction of epinephrine. The GQD can make the surface of the electrode more conductive and the negatively charged carboxyl groups existing at their surfaces not only result in increased stability but also interact with amine functional groups in the epinephrine structure through an electrostatic interaction. Meanwhile, at GQD–CS-CPE (e), the intensity of redox peaks increased considerably. The use of GQD–CS as a modifier obviously improved the electrochemical redox behavior of epinephrine. Hence, this modified electrode based on GQD and chitosan can be used as an excellent electrochemical platform for determining traces of the epinephrine.
The CS is well known as a cationic biological macromolecule containing primary amines so that enables keeping of the GQD at the electrode via electrostatic and hydrogen bonding interactions [30].

Square wave voltammograms in Fig. 5B obviously show that the oxidation current of EP is higher at the modified electrode than that at the bare electrode, and the oxidation peak potential is shifted more negatively than that at the bare electrode. These improvements can be resulted from the electrocatalytic ability of modified electrode toward EP electrooxidation. Compared to the bare CPE, CS-CPE, and GQD-CPE, the GQD-CS-CPE modified electrode possessed larger surface areas, higher conductivity, uniform structure, and good biocompatibility.

Since the electrochemical reactions of many organic compounds involve at least one exchangeable proton, the pH of the solution is expected to possess a significant impact on their reaction speed. Therefore, the effect of pH of the solution on the electrochemical response of EP was investigated at different pH values of a phosphate buffer solution, in the range of 4.0 to 9.0, by CV and the results are showed in Fig. S2A and the corresponding plot of peak currents against pH are showed in Fig. S2B.

The anodic peak current of EP increased with the increasing of solution pH until pH reached 7.0; then it decreased with increasing pH. This event can be explained by using pH diagram of EP in Scheme S1. In acidic solution, chitosan and GQDs are easy to be protonated and the static repulsion action between EP cation and protonated modifier at the surface of electrode decreases and the anodic peak current increase with increasing the pH. On the other hand, in neutral solution, nonprotonated form or negative form at the electrode and the positive charge of epinephrine enhances electrostatic interaction. After pH 7.0, the current signal decreases due to the charge of epinephrine and negative form of modifier. The largest current signal was obtained at pH 7.0 and, therefore, this pH was selected for the sensitive determination of epinephrine. In addition, when the pH was increased, peak potential shifted to lower potentials (Fig. S2C), so that the peak potential E Pa was found to be linear with pH values over the range of 4.0–9.0 with a linear regression equation of E pa (mV) = −58.114 pH + 669.33 (R2 = 0.9901) and a slope value of −58.029 mV pH −1. These results demonstrated that the number of transferred electrons in the processes is equal to the number of protons. These conclusions are in accordance with the mechanisms of EP electrochemical reactions as shown in Scheme. 1 , which is consistent with the previously reported mechanism [31, 32]. As it is obvious in Fig. S2C the anodic peak potentials were strongly dependent on pH suggesting the participation of H+ in the oxidation processes. Therefore, increasing the pH can accelerate the oxidation reaction of epinephrine according to the following mechanism in Scheme. 1 and is expected to move the oxidation peak potential of epinephrine toward more negative potentials.

In order to investigate the kinetics of epinephrine oxidation reaction on the surface of GQD-CS modified electrode, the influence of scan rate on the cyclic voltammograms was studied and the results are showed in Fig. 6A. The results showed that the peak current (Ip) is directly proportional to the scan rate (ν) in the range of 10–100 mV s−1 (Fig. 6B). Moreover, there is a linear relationship between the peak current (Ip) and the square root of the scan rate (ν1/2) over the scan rates of 10 to 500 mV s−1 for the EP (Fig. 6C), which indicates that in addition to the existence of a surface controlled reaction mechanism, a diffusion-controlled mechanism is dominated for EP electrochemical reactions at higher scan rates. This phenomenon confirms that GQD-CS CPE possesses a faster charge transfer kinetics that can follow the higher scan rates. The logarithm of the current was also plotted in term of the logarithm of scan rate (Fig. S3), and discussed in more details in the Supplementary section.

The effect of common co-existing substances was investigated. Whenever the relative error exceeded 5%, the corresponding substance was considered as an interfering agent. The experiments showed that most ions such as Na+, K+, Cl−, NO3 −, SO4 2− (500 fold), Ca2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Zn2+, Al3+ (100 fold) caused only negligible change. In the case of coexisting biological compounds, including glucose, sucrose, fructose, cysteine, aspartic acid, alanine, glutamic acid, histidine, arginine, glycine and, tryptophan, we found that none of them has any interference effect up to 100 fold in epinephrine determination. More discussion is available in Supplementary S3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calibration plot, limit of detection reproducibility, and repeatability
ce:section.ce:para: We used hydrodynamic chronoamperometry as a simple and sensitive electrochemical method for the determination of EP. The chronoamperograms for stepwise injections of small amounts of EP into 10 mL of the stirred solution of PB (pH 7.0) at an applied potential of 0.330 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) were obtained and showed in Fig. 7A. As it is obvious, during the successive addition of EP, at the optimum conditions, well-defined current responses have been observed. The resulting calibration curve revealed a good linear relationship between oxidation currents and concentration of EP (Fig. 7B) in the range of 0.360 to 380 μM with the regression equation of I (μA) = 41,296 [EP] (M) + 1.0204 (R2 = 0.9967) and a limit of detection of 0.3 nM (3Sb/m).

In order to evaluate the analytical characteristics of the modified electrode for the determination of EP, they were compared with those of the different modified electrodes previously reported in the literature and the results have been summarized in Table 1 . As you can see, the linear range of the developed modified electrode is improved in some cases and is comparable with some of them. Some of the electrodes suffer from interference species such as ascorbic acid, dopamine, and uric acid while these chemicals have not been interfered with at the surface of GQD-CS modified electrode. Moreover, some electrodes are expensive and the steps of modification is time-consuming and also some modifiers have very complicated structures and synthesis routes compare to GQD-CS modified electrode which is a simple, cheap, stable, renewable, biocompatible, and new nanoplatform that can be applied for EP determination [33, 34] and in other electrochemical systems.

The reproducibility of this modified electrode was estimated by comparing the oxidation peak current of 0.5 mM epinephrine. The relative standard deviation (RSD) for the CV peak currents of EP, based on 6 replicates, was 2.8%, indicating good repeatability of the modified electrode. In addition, five modified electrodes fabricated independently were used to investigate the electrochemical response of EP, under the same experimental conditions, and the RSD obtained was 3.4%, revealing a suitable reproducibility in the electrode preparation. The electrode also showed a high stability of nearly over 1 month storing in the air.

In order to evaluate the applicability of the method, the modified electrode was applied to the EP determination in blood serum and in epinephrine hydrochloride injection solution, as real samples. Typically, 10.0 mL of diluted blood serum, including phosphate buffer of pH 7.0, was transferred to an electrolytic cell and the samples were spiked with specified concentrations of EP. Then, the chronoamperomograms were recorded and the results have been showed in Table S2. The results showed a good performance of the modified electrode and indicated that the present method was accurate and reliable for practical samples.

In this study, a new electrochemical sensor based on the GQD–chitosan bulk-modified carbon paste electrode has been developed and characterized. The presence of chitosan with GQD has been found to improve the stability of the modified electrode. The electrochemical oxidation of epinephrine improved at GQD–chitosan modified CPE, has been presented excellent electrocatalytic properties and improved results including large current response, low charge transfer resistance, lower overpotential, low detection limit and good reproducibility. The modified electrode has been successfully applied to the EP determination in real samples. Meanwhile, the outstanding electrochemical and electrocatalytic function and inherent biocompatibility have made the GQD-chitosan carbon paste electrode a promising nanoplatform to construct the new electrochemical sensors and biosensors. It has provided a route to construct paste modified electrode with other material or quantum dots especially the quantum dots that are soluble and have leakage potential.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the support of this work by Shiraz University Research Council (grant number 93GCU2M172254).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.molliq.2018.06.093.
